title: Stacks, Queues, and Binary Search
description: Practical mental models for classic interview tools that look harder than they really are.

cards.question: When should I think about a stack in a coding problem?
cards.shortAnswer: When the most recent unresolved item is the one I need to handle next.
cards.expectedAnswer: A stack is a good fit when the problem naturally behaves like last-in, first-out. That usually means I need to remember the most recent unresolved thing, such as open brackets, previous elements waiting for a greater value, or nested structure that must be closed in reverse order.
keyPoints: common in parentheses and monotonic stack problems
commonTraps: using a stack when plain iteration is enough
not understanding what the stack is storing and why
followUps: How is a stack different from recursion here?

cards.question: When should I think about binary search?
cards.shortAnswer: When the search space is ordered or when I can test a condition that changes from false to true in one direction.
cards.expectedAnswer: Binary search is not only for finding a number in a sorted array. It is useful whenever the search space has order and I can check a condition that flips in one direction, such as false-then-true or true-then-false. The key is that I can throw away half of the remaining options after each check.
commonTraps: thinking binary search only works on arrays
not defining the condition clearly before coding
followUps: What is binary search on answer?
How do you avoid off-by-one mistakes?
